{{short description|American social psychologist and writer}}
{{other people||Tim Wilson (disambiguation)}}
{{Use mdy dates|date=November 2022}}

{{Infobox scientist
| fields            = [[Social psychology]]
| workplaces        = [[University of Virginia]]
| alma_mater        = [[Hampshire College]] ([[Bachelor of Arts|BA]])<br>[[University of Michigan]] ([[Master of Arts|MA]], [[Doctor of Philosophy|PhD]])
| thesis_title      = Awareness and self-perception
| thesis_url        = https://www.proquest.com/docview/302868164/
| thesis_year       = 1977
| doctoral_advisor  = [[Richard E. Nisbett|Richard Nisbett]]
}}

'''Timothy DeCamp Wilson''' is an American [[Social psychology|social psychologist]] and writer. 

He is the Sherrell J. Aston Professor of [[Psychology]] at the [[University of Virginia]] and teaches public policy at the [[Frank Batten School of Leadership and Public Policy]]. He is known for his research on [[Self-knowledge (psychology)|self-knowledge]] and the influence of the [[unconscious mind]] on [[decision-making]], [[preferences]] and [[behavior]]. He is the author of two popular books on psychology, ''Strangers to Ourselves: Discovering the Adaptive Unconscious'' and ''Redirect: The Surprising New Science of Psychological Change.''

== Education ==
Wilson received a [[Bachelor of Arts]] with a major in psychology from [[Hampshire College]] in 1973. He received a [[Master of Arts]] in 1975 and a [[Doctor of Philosophy]] in 1977, both in psychology from the [[University of Michigan]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Timothy Wilson |url=https://batten.virginia.edu/people/timothy-wilson |access-date=2023-03-04 |website=Frank Batten School of Leadership and Public Policy {{!}} University of Virginia |language=en}}</ref>

== Career ==
Wilson is best known for his research on the [[adaptive unconscious]], [[self-knowledge (psychology)|self-knowledge]], and [[affective forecasting]]. With [[Richard Nisbett]], Wilson authored one of psychology's most cited papers, "Telling more than we can know {{ndash}} verbal reports on mental processes," that demonstrated the difficulty humans have in introspecting on their own mental processes (''[[Psychological Review]]'', 1977,<ref name="Telling">{{cite journal|date=May 1977|title=Telling more than we can know: Verbal reports on mental processes|url=https://deepblue.lib.umich.edu/bitstream/handle/2027.42/92167/TellingMoreThanWeCanKnow.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y|journal=[[Psychological Review]]|volume=84|issue=3|pages=231–259|doi=10.1037/0033-295X.84.3.231|author=Nisbett, Richard E.; Wilson, Timothy D.|hdl=2027.42/92167|hdl-access=free}}</ref> cited 2731 times as of May 22, 2007, according to [[ISI Web of Knowledge]]). His longtime collaborator is [[Daniel Gilbert (psychologist)|Daniel Gilbert]] of [[Harvard University]].

Wilson has published two popular press books, ''Strangers to Ourselves'' and ''Redirect'', and co-authored ''Social Psychology,'' an introductory textbook on [[social psychology]]. The textbook has been translated into Italian, Polish, Chinese, German, Russian, and Serbian; ''Strangers to Ourselves'' has been translated into Dutch and Japanese, with Chinese and German editions forthcoming.

Wilson received a Bachelor of Arts in [[psychology]] from [[Hampshire College]] in 1973 and a PhD in [[social psychology]] from the [[University of Michigan]]'s [[Princeton University Department of Psychology|Department of Psychology]] in 1977. From 1977 to 1979, he was a faculty member at [[Duke University]]. Since 1979, he has been a faculty member at the [[University of Virginia]]. His research has been supported by the [[National Institute of Mental Health]], the [[National Science Foundation]], and the [[Russell Sage Foundation]].  In 2009, he was named as a fellow to the [[American Academy of Arts and Sciences]].

His work has received numerous awards, including an All-University Outstanding Teaching Award from the [[University of Virginia]] in 2001, as well as the Thomas Jefferson Award for Excellence in Scholarship in 2015. In 2013, he was the recipient of the [[Society for Personality and Social Psychology]]'s Donald T. Campbell Award, and in 2015 he was named a William James Fellow by the [[Association for Psychological Science]].

He lives in [[Charlottesville, Virginia]], with his wife, Deirdre Smith. He has two children, Christopher and Leigh.
